text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform grafting and reconstruction on the inferior vena cava, with precise manipulation of instruments visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs segmental resection of the inferior vena cava (IVC) followed by anastomosis of a tubularized PTFE vasculature graft. In the second case, an eroded IVC filter is extracted, and a PTFE vasculature patch graft is anastomosed to the IVC.

In the first case, we describe a seventy-eight year old male with a ten centimeter T-three B right renal mass. The patient experienced associated right lower extremity claudication symptoms due to IVC involvement of the tumor. and right lower extremity pain and weakness due to lymphadenopathy and bulky tumor effect, invading into the paraspinous and posterior lateral abdominal wall structures. The patient elected to undergo robotic right radical nephrectomy and IVC tumor thrombectomy.

The patient is positioned in a right side up modified flank position. A 12mm assistant umbilical port is placed first, followed by four robotic ports aligned in a straight line along the midclavicular paramedian lines, each spaced eight centimeters apart.

The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava after mobilizing the ascending colon from Girota's fascia and medially cocorizing the duodenum. A laparoscopic intraoperative ultrasound probe surveys the IVC, revealing thrombus in the suprarenal IVC. The right renal artery is identified in the inter-aorta cava location and divided using non-absorbable clips to avoid manipulation of the right renal vein and tumor thrombus.

The surgeon applies bulldog vascular clamps to the contralateral renal vein, infrarenal IVC, and suprarenal IVC. A cavotomy is made along the anterior surface of the IVC, revealing tumor invasion into the IVC wall and lumen of the posterior lumbar branches. A 5.5 cm segment of the IVC is resected due to clot burden and tumor invasion. A 6 cm tubularized synthetic polyester graft is anastomosed to the cephalad portion of the native IVC using PTFE non-absorbable monofilament suture in a bidirectional fashion.

The surgeon anastomoses the graft to the caudal portion of the native inferior vena cava (IVC) using IV-O PTFE non-absorbable monofilament suture in a bidirectional fashion. The infrarenal bulldog clamp is briefly opened to flush out air and clots from the reconstructed area before being reapplied. The anastomosis is completed, and the bulldog clamps are sequentially removed, showing no evidence of graft leakage. An intraoperative ultrasound confirms the patency of the graft and the superior and inferior portions of the vena cava.

Regarding perioperative findings, the IVC was cross clamped for one hundred eight minutes. Total consult time was three hundred seventy two minutes and estimated blood loss was one point seven liters. The patient stayed in the hospital for six days due to postoperative hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring extended non-rebreather oxygen ventilation. The patient was discharged home and did not experience any vascular complication postoperatively.

In the second case, we described a forty-three-year-old female with an extruded IVC filter. Preoperative CT imaging showed that the infrarenal IVC filter had eroded outside of the IVC. The shape of the Opti's filter is shown here. Upon direct inspection of the IVC, it was clear that the IVC filter had eroded through the wall of the IVC. The patient underwent full therapeutic heparinization in anticipation of extended IVC clamp times.

The inferior vena cava (IVC) is cross-clamped, and a longitudinal cavotomy is made along its anterior surface. The IVC filter is visibly embedded within the vena cava, with broken prongs intruding into the vessel wall. The surgeon begins to remove the filter piece by piece.

After removing all visible pieces of the IVC filter, the surgeon resects the desmoplastic reaction along the anterior wall of the vena cava to eliminate excess calcifications. The cavotomy measures eight centimeters. A synthetic patch graft is anastomosed to the defect using 5-0 PTFE non-absorbable monofilament suture in a running fashion. The medial aspect is anastomosed first, followed by the lateral aspect, ensuring a watertight closure.

With regards to perioperative findings, the IVC was cross-clamped for one hundred thirty-two minutes. Total consult time was two hundred forty-five minutes and estimated blood loss was three hundred milliliters. The patient was discharged in five days after an uneventful hospital course. At two year follow-up, The patient has not experienced any major complications and her most recent CT venogram demonstrated a widely patent IVC without any thrombus formation.

As the utilization of robotic surgery continues to expand, we demonstrate that robotic urologic surgeons can perform vascular reconstruction safely and effectively for management of various IVC pathologies.
